FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data with 11,878 9-10 year-old participants from 21 research sites in the United States

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: social media addiction questionnaire (SMAQ), video game addiction questionnaire (VGAQ), screen time by modality (video games, online social activity, online dating, sleep screen use, bedtime phone use)

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests: longitudinal changes in screen usage via mixed-effects models and ANOVA; psychometric analyses (CFA) with χ² tests; comparisons across sex, race/ethnicity groups with reported p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: p-values (p<.001 for sex/race differences in SMAQ and VGAQ), χ² values (e.g., χ²(9)=597.23, p<.001), factor model fit indices (CFI=0.943, RMSEA=0.109), means and SDs for SMAQ/VGAQ by demographic groups

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal measurement study describing psychometric properties of SMAQ and VGAQ with demographic comparisons across baseline-year 2

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because: (1) paper includes cross-sectional demographic comparisons with screen/SM variables; (2) paper focuses on measurement invariance and psychometric properties rather than directionality of causation; (3) primary analyses are descriptive/psychometric in nature, not prospective prediction of future SM use from baseline demographics alone
notes: E5 applies: measurement paper that reports SM↔non-SM associations (screen use differences by sex/race). E8 applies: includes inferential statistical tests (χ², mixed-effects ANOVA) beyond descriptive means/SDs.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from 21 sites; sample of 2217 children (ages 9-10 years) from ABCD 2.0.1 release baseline assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/video gaming variable: self-reported time spent on video gaming (hours per week); stratified by heavy gamers (≥21 hours/week) vs non-gamers (0 hours/week)

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests linking video gaming to cognitive outcomes: comparison of fMRI task performance (n-back, stop signal) and BOLD signal between video gamers and non-video gamers with statistical comparisons

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: sample sizes (n=1128 non-gamers, n=679 gamers for stop signal task; n=1278 non-gamers, n=800 gamers for n-back), behavioral performance differences between groups, BOLD signal differences in brain regions

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: case-control study comparing cognitive performance and fMRI BOLD signal between video gamers and non-video gamers

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in JAMA Network Open, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because video gaming is the independent variable predicting cognitive/neuroimaging outcomes (not the dependent variable); individual behavioral/cognitive predictors are involved
notes: E7 applies: video gaming as independent predictor of non-SM outcome (cognitive performance/BOLD signal).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from more than 3,000 young adolescents (11-14 years) in the ongoing Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time as a predictor: 'more screen time' identified as significant psychosocial factor predicting youth psychological distress during COVID-19 pandemic

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports machine learning model results predicting psychological distress; screen time included as one of multiple significant predictors in classifier models

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract mentions that screen time was a significant predictor but does not provide specific numeric coefficients, odds ratios, or p-values for the screen time predictor individually

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal study applying machine learning models to identify predictors of psychological distress in youth during pandemic

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in The Journal of Adolescent Health, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because: (1) screen time is one predictor among many in a machine learning model; (2) paper includes individual psychological/behavioral predictors (anxiety, depressive symptoms, sleep problems, internalizing symptoms); (3) screen time→distress is tested alongside psychological factors, not just demographics
notes: E4 applies: ML model reports classifier performance but does not provide individual coefficient for screen time predictor. Full PDF needed to confirm whether numeric coefficients exist in supplement tables.
